rows.v: Whiteleg (Farmed) Shrimp, Wild Gulf Shrimp, Tiger Prawn, Spot Prawn, Rock Shrimp, Royal Red Shrimp
Quick-cooking and versatile — grill, sauté, boil, or use raw in crudo.
Shrimp is the most-eaten seafood in the United States — and the line between 'shrimp' and 'prawn' is mostly about size and biology, not flavor; cooks use the words almost interchangeably.
American (Maine) Lobster, Spiny (Rock) Lobster, Langoustine, Crawfish, Slipper Lobster
Simple preparations — steaming or boiling lets the sweet, delicate meat shine.
Lobster was once so common it was fed to prisoners and servants, who reportedly complained — it only became a luxury in the 20th century as it grew scarce and fashionable.
King Crab, Dungeness Crab, Snow Crab, Blue Crab, Soft-Shell Crab, Stone Crab, Peekytoe Crab
Sweet and delicate — best with light seasoning that doesn't mask the natural flavor.

A stone crab is one of the few sustainable seafoods you can harvest without killing the animal — fishermen take a single claw and return the crab, which regrows the missing claw within about 18 months.
Pacific Oyster, Atlantic (Eastern) Oyster, Kumamoto Oyster, Olympia Oyster, European Flat (Belon), Sydney Rock Oyster
Served raw on the half shell, or lightly cooked — minimal handling preserves their character.
An oyster's flavor comes from its 'merroir' — the marine version of terroir — which is why a single species can taste sweet and mild in one bay and intensely briny in another.
Littleneck Clam, Cherrystone Clam, Quahog, Manila Clam, Razor Clam, Geoduck
Steamed in broth, wine, or sauce — the cooking liquid becomes part of the dish.

The old rule to eat shellfish only in 'R' months (September–April) comes from warm-weather 'red tides,' algal blooms that make wild shellfish unsafe; modern farmed clams in monitored waters are safe year-round.
Blue Mussel, Green-Lipped Mussel, Mediterranean Mussel, Sea Scallop, Bay Scallop, Cockle
Mussels steam quickly in flavorful liquid; scallops sear best in a hot, dry pan.
With seared scallops, dryness is everything — 'dry' (untreated) scallops brown into a golden crust, while 'wet' ones soaked in preservative just steam and never sear.
Squid (Calamari), Octopus, Cuttlefish, Baby Octopus
Quick high-heat searing or long slow braising — anything in between turns them rubbery.
Squid and octopus have a narrow cooking window — cook them either very fast (seconds) or very slow (a long braise); anything in between turns them to rubber.
Abalone, Conch, Whelk, Periwinkle, Sea Urchin (Uni), Sea Cucumber
Adventurous textures — often slow-cooked, tenderized, or served in small, intensely flavored bites.
Sea urchin is technically neither a fish nor a shellfish but an echinoderm — a spiny relative of the starfish — and the buttery 'uni' prized on sushi is actually its reproductive organs.
